1: The angel descending from heaven with the key to the bottomless pit (Revelation 20:1) operates under Christ's authority — Christ himself "has the keys of Death and Hades" (Revelation 1:18). The binding of Satan for a thousand years enacts what Christ announced in advance: "I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven" (Luke 10:18) and "Now will the ruler of this world be cast out" (John 12:31). The Christus Victor theology that runs throughout Revelation reaches a penultimate expression here: the devil's power is bound precisely because Christ has conquered him through death and resurrection.
2: Satan is identified with four names in Revelation 20:2: the dragon (Revelation 12:3-4), the ancient serpent (Genesis 3:1-15), the devil (Greek: slanderer), and Satan (Hebrew: adversary). All four names appear together in 12:9 at his expulsion from heaven. The thousand-year binding is read against the background of 1 Enoch Revelation 10:4-6 (Azazel/Semyaza bound in preparation for final judgment), Jude 6, and 2 Peter 2:4 — a Second Temple tradition that Christ's work now enacts definitively for the chief adversary.
3: Satan is sealed in the pit "so that he might not deceive the nations any longer" (Revelation 20:3). His primary activity throughout Revelation is deception — 12:9 names it explicitly: "the deceiver of the whole world." Christ by contrast is "Faithful and True" (Revelation 19:11) and the "faithful and true witness" (Revelation 3:14). The millennium is the period in which the nations are freed from Satan's wholesale deception — the mission of the church to all nations (Matthew 28:19-20) takes place within this window of bound deception. The "must be released" (Revelation 20:3) is God's sovereign permission for the final testing.
4: "They came to life and reigned with Christ for a thousand years" (Revelation 20:4) — the "first resurrection" that grants the saints co-reign with Christ. The pattern follows Pauline language: those who have died with Christ are raised with Christ (Romans 6:3-5; Colossians 2:12; 2 Timothy 2:11-12 "if we have died with him, we will also live with him; if we endure, we will also reign with him"). Those specifically in view are those who were beheaded for their testimony (martyria) of Jesus — the martyr-witnesses whose cause was vindicated in Revelation 18-19. They now share Christ's enthronement (Revelation 3:21; Ephesians 2:6).
5: "The rest of the dead did not come to life until the thousand years were ended. This is the first resurrection" (Revelation 20:5). The "first resurrection" is distinguished from the resurrection of the rest of the dead (Revelation 20:12-13), suggesting two resurrections — a reading that goes back to 1 Corinthians 15:20-24 ("Christ the firstfruits, then at his coming those who belong to Christ, then comes the end"). Christ himself is the first resurrection (John 11:25: "I am the resurrection and the life"), and those who share in him share in his resurrection-life now (John 5:24-25; Colossians 3:1).
6: "Blessed and holy is the one who shares in the first resurrection" (Revelation 20:6) is the fifth beatitude (cf. Revelation 1:3; Revelation 14:13; Revelation 16:15; Revelation 19:9; Revelation 22:7, 14). Those who share in it are priests of God and of Christ — the royal priesthood of 1 Peter 2:9, Exodus 19:6, and Revelation 1:6. "Over such the second death has no power" — because those who have died with Christ in baptism (Romans 6:3-4) and through martyrdom are already on the resurrection side of death. The second death (Revelation 20:14: the lake of fire) has no jurisdiction over those who have already passed through death into Christ's life.
7: Satan's release after the thousand years (Revelation 20:7) is not an escape but a sovereign permission ("must be released," Revelation 20:3) — the "little while" of his second freedom corresponds to the "little while" of his earlier fury after expulsion from heaven (Revelation 12:12). The deliberate echo signals that the final deception follows the same pattern as the persecution of the saints: intensified pressure followed by definitive defeat. God permits the final deception precisely to expose every remaining opposition and to complete the separation.
8: Gog and Magog (Revelation 20:8) — the eschatological cipher drawn from Ezek 38-39, where Gog of the land of Magog leads a coalition against restored Israel. By John's time the Gog-Magog tradition had become a flexible symbol for the final, comprehensive attack of the nations on God's people (Qumran War Scroll 1QM; b. Sanhedrin 97b; m. Eduyot Revelation 2:10). The "four corners of the earth" expresses geographic totality — this is a universal final assault, not a regional conflict. The contrast with the Lamb's gathering of nations to new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:24-26) could not be sharper.
9: "Fire came down from heaven and consumed them" (Revelation 20:9) — divine fire without any instrument or human army. The echo is of Elijah's fire from heaven (1 Kings 18:38; 2 Kings 1:10-12) and the Sodom judgment (Genesis 19:24), but more immediately of Ezekiel 38:22 and Revelation 39:6 (fire and sulfur on Gog). The "beloved city" (Revelation 20:9) echoes Psalms 78:68 and Revelation 87:2 (Jerusalem as beloved) but in context refers to the camp of the saints — the messianic community that is the locus of God's presence. The fire comes down from heaven because Christ is enthroned in heaven (Revelation 1:13-16; Revelation 3:21) and the judgment flows from his throne.
10: The devil thrown into the lake of fire (Revelation 20:10) — the same destiny as the beast and false prophet (Revelation 19:20), now joined by their instigator. "Tormented day and night forever and ever" uses the same formula as the smoke of Babylon (Revelation 19:3) and the smoke of those who take the beast's mark (Revelation 14:11). The finality is the point: this is not an intermediate state but the permanent destination of the adversary, whose career of deception is permanently ended. The Christological significance is that Christ's victory at the cross over the "ruler of this world" (John 12:31; Colossians 2:15) is here finally, visibly consummated.
11: The great white throne (Revelation 20:11) from which "earth and sky fled away" echoes Daniel 7:9-10 (the Ancient of Days enthroned) and 1 Enoch Revelation 47:3. The fleeing of earth and heaven is the dissolution of the present cosmos in preparation for the new creation (Revelation 21:1). The judge seated on the throne is identified implicitly with Christ throughout Revelation — the Father has entrusted all judgment to the Son (John 5:22, 27; Acts 17:31; Romans 2:16; 2 Corinthians 5:10; 2 Timothy 4:1). The white throne is the Lamb's throne (Revelation 22:1, 3: "the throne of God and of the Lamb").
12: The dead "small and great" standing before the throne (Revelation 20:12) echoes the social catalogue of those who bore the beast's mark (Revelation 13:16) — no status or position grants exemption from this judgment. Two sets of books are opened: the books of deeds, and the book of life. The book of life is the Lamb's book (Revelation 21:27: "the book of life of the Lamb who was slain") — those written in it are those purchased by the Lamb's blood (Revelation 5:9). The two books represent two bases of judgment: works and grace, held simultaneously in the Lamb's judicial act.
13: "The sea gave up the dead who were in it, Death and Hades gave up the dead who were in them" (Revelation 20:13) — the universal scope of resurrection. No mode of death, no location of burial, puts a human being beyond the reach of the Lamb who "has the keys of Death and Hades" (Revelation 1:18). The sea in apocalyptic represents chaos and unreachable depth (Job 38:16-17; Psalms 139:8-10); even the sea's dead are recoverable. This is the cosmic fulfillment of Jesus's promise: "All that the Father gives me will come to me" (John 6:37) and "I will raise it up on the last day" (John 6:39-40).
14: "Then Death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire. This is the second death, the lake of fire" (Revelation 20:14). The death of Death fulfills 1 Corinthians 15:26 ("the last enemy to be destroyed is death") and Isaiah 25:8 ("he will swallow up death forever"). Christ who "holds the keys of Death and Hades" (Revelation 1:18) now permanently locks those powers in the lake of fire. Hosea 13:14 (cited in 1 Corinthians 15:55-56: "O death, where is your victory?") is the prophetic basis: the Christ-event that Paul celebrates as accomplished proleptically in the resurrection is here shown as consummated cosmically. Death is not merely overcome but destroyed.
15: "If anyone's name was not found written in the book of life, he was thrown into the lake of fire" (Revelation 20:15) — the book of life is the Lamb's book (Revelation 21:27), written before the foundation of the world (Revelation 13:8; Revelation 17:8; cf. Luke 10:20; Philippians 4:3; Hebrews 12:23). The judgment is therefore not arbitrary but follows from each person's relationship to the Lamb whose blood purchased those in the book (Revelation 5:9). The second death has no power over those in the first resurrection (Revelation 20:6), who are those in the Lamb's book — the two descriptions are coextensive. The lake of fire is the permanent separation from the Lamb's presence that those who rejected him chose (2 Thessalonians 1:9).
